1: vayeikh ish mibet Levi vayiqach et-bat Levi (wayyēleḵ ʾîš mibbet lēwî wayyiqqaḥ ʾet-bat lēwî): 'A man from the house of Levi went and married a daughter of Levi.' The anonymity of Moses's parents — they are not named until Exodus 6:20 (Amram and Jochebed) — focuses attention on the child rather than the lineage. Moses is from the tribe designated for priestly service (Levi), establishing his priestly identity before his prophetic commission. The double Levi (from Levi, daughter of Levi) underscores the covenantal continuity from the patriarchs.
2: vatare oto ki-tov hu (watteʾreʾ ʾōtô kî-ṭôb hûʾ): 'She saw that he was good.' Ki-tov (that it was good): the precise phrase from the creation narrative (Genesis 1:4, 10, 12, 18, 21, 25, 31 — God saw that it was good). Moses's mother recognizes in her infant the same goodness that YHWH recognized in creation. Stephen in Acts 7:20 uses asteios (beautiful/fair before God), and Hebrews 11:23 calls him asteion — the parents saw something God-given in the child. The creation-goodness language signals that Moses's birth is a new creative act.
3: teva gome vatachmeraha bachemor uvazafet (tēbat gōme wattaḥmĕrāh baḥēmār ûbazzāpet): 'A basket of bulrushes/papyrus, she plastered it with bitumen and pitch.' Teva (H8392, ark/chest): the precise word used for Noah's ark (Genesis 6:14-16) — only these two occurrences in the entire OT. Moses's basket is a miniature ark: like Noah, he is preserved in a teva through water while death surrounds. The pitch-and-bitumen coating echoes Noah's ark (Genesis 6:14 — cover it with pitch inside and out). Both arks carry the seed of a new beginning through the waters of judgment.
4: vateytsatazev achoto merachok lede'ah mah-ye'aseh lo: 'His sister stood at a distance to know what would be done for him.' Moses's unnamed sister (later Miriam, Exodus 15:20) watches from a distance — a combination of care and prudential distance. The word lede'ah (to know, from yada) — she watches to know what will happen. Covenant protection often works through attentive human witness, the small acts of faithfulness that shelter the vulnerable.
5: vateredu bat-Paroh lirchots al-hayeor: 'The daughter of Pharaoh came down to bathe at the Nile.' Pharaoh's own daughter, drawing from the Nile to which Hebrew sons were condemned, discovers and rescues the condemned child. The irony is structural: the instrument of death (the Nile, Pharaoh's decree) becomes the location of rescue, and the agent of rescue is from within the house of the oppressor. YHWH's providential sovereignty uses the empire's own household against its own decree.
6: vatiftech vatirehu et-hayeled vehinei naar boche vatachemel alav: 'She opened it and saw the child — a boy crying; and she had compassion on him.' Vatachemel alav (she had compassion/pity on him): from chamal (H2550, have compassion/spare) — the spontaneous movement of the heart toward the suffering vulnerable. This compassion from within Pharaoh's own household is the surprise of grace: deliverance comes from unexpected places. The compassion of the divine is here mediated through a pagan princess.
7: vatomer achoto el-bat Paroh haelich veqarati lach isha meineket min-haivriyot: 'His sister said to Pharaoh's daughter: Shall I go and call you a nurse from the Hebrew women?' The sister's intervention brings the child back to his own mother — he is nursed by his biological mother at Pharaoh's expense. Providence arranges that Moses receives his Hebrew identity at his mother's breast, even while living in Pharaoh's household. The covenant heritage is transmitted through the most intimate possible means.
8: vateleich ha'alma vatiqra et-em hayaled: 'The young woman went and called the child's mother.' The chain of protection — mother, sister, princess — is the web of feminine courage that saves Moses. Multiple women act against the death-decree of a single man: the midwives (Exodus 1), Moses's mother, his sister, Pharaoh's daughter. The masculine power of empire is subverted by the feminine power of compassion. The Exodus begins with the courage of women.
9: vatomer bat-Paroh helikhihu et-hayeled hazeh vehenikiehu li vaani etten et-secharch: 'Take this child and nurse him for me, and I will give you your wages.' Moses's mother is paid to nurse her own son. The irony of empire: Pharaoh's household funds the formation of its own deliverer. YHWH consistently uses the resources of the oppressor to equip those who will liberate the oppressed (cf. Israel's plundering of Egypt in Exodus 12:35-36).
10: vatiqra shemo Mosheh vatomer ki min-hamayim meshitihu (wattiqrāʾ šĕmô mōšeh wattōʾmer kî-min-hammayim mĕšîtîhû): 'She called his name Moses, saying: for I drew him out of the water.' Mosheh (H4872) from mashah (H4871, draw out): the name-etymology given by Pharaoh's daughter — the Egyptian princess naming the Hebrew child after the act of rescue. A Hebrew passive form would mean one who is drawn out; an active form, one who draws out. Both work: Moses is drawn out of the Nile and will draw Israel out of Egypt. The name is a vocation compressed into a sound.
11: vayehi bayamim hahem vayigdal Mosheh vayetze el-echav vayar besivolotam: 'In those days, when Moses had grown up, he went out to his brothers and looked at their burdens.' Vayar besivolotam (he saw their burdens): Moses's seeing echoes YHWH's seeing in Exodus 3:7 (ra'o raiti et-oni ammi). The prophet who will mediate YHWH's deliverance first personally sees what YHWH has seen: the suffering of the covenant people. Stephen in Acts 7:23-24 identifies this as Moses's deliberate identification with his people, parallel to Christ's incarnational identification with human suffering (Hebrews 2:17-18).
12: vayifen ko vako vayar ki ein ish vayak et-haMitsri: 'He looked this way and that, and seeing no one, he struck the Egyptian.' The furtive glance before action — the consciousness of risk even in righteous response to injustice. Moses acts to defend the oppressed but through human agency that will need to be transformed. His zeal is real but his method (secret violence) is insufficient for the scale of liberation required. The Exodus will not be achieved by a single man's sword but by YHWH's mighty hand.
13: vayetze bayom hasheni vehinei shnei anashim ivrim nitsim: 'On the second day he went out and saw two Hebrew men quarreling.' The internal conflict within the oppressed community — the violence of empire internalized among its victims. The divided Hebrew community that rejects its own deliverer anticipates the pattern of Israel's repeated rejection of Moses in the wilderness (Exod 16-17; Num 14) and ultimately of the prophet like Moses (Acts 7:35-39).
14: mi samcha le'ish sar veshofet aleinu (mî śāmĕkā lĕʾîš śar wĕšōpēṭ ʿālênû): 'Who made you a prince and a judge over us?' The Hebrew slave's challenge to Moses's authority is quoted by Stephen in Acts 7:35 as typological of Israel's rejection of Christ: this Moses, whom they rejected saying who made you a ruler and judge? — this man God sent as both ruler and redeemer. The rejection of Moses the deliverer prefigures the rejection of the deliverer Moses pointed to.
15: vayishma Paroh et-hadavar hazeh vayevaqesh lahrog et-Mosheh vayivarch Mosheh mipnei Paroh: 'Pharaoh heard this thing and sought to kill Moses, and Moses fled from Pharaoh's face.' The exile of Moses to Midian is both flight from imperial violence and the necessary preparation for the burning-bush commission. The wilderness period (forty years, Acts 7:30) shapes the deliverer before deployment. Jesus's forty days in the wilderness (Matthew 4:1-11) compresses the Mosaic forty-year preparation into forty days of tested faithfulness.
16: ulekhohen Midyan sheva banot: 'The priest of Midian had seven daughters.' Moses encounters the daughters of Jethro (Reuel) at the well — the third great well-meeting in Genesis-Exodus (Isaac-Rebekah, Gen 24; Jacob-Rachel, Gen 29; now Moses-Zipporah). Wells are covenant meeting places in the OT: the place where the wandering stranger finds the one who will become the link to his future. Moses is now a stranger without a people — the well scene marks his transition from Egyptian prince to Midianite sojourner.
17: vayaqom Mosheh vayoshien vayashq et-tsonam: 'Moses stood up and helped them and watered their flock.' Moses's defense of the women against the hostile shepherds reveals his character: he uses his strength on behalf of the vulnerable. The shepherd who defends his flock — against hostile shepherds, against beasts — is the role Moses will now learn by tending Jethro's flock and that he will exercise for Israel for forty years in the wilderness. The Good Shepherd's character is forged in Midian.
18: madu'a mihartem bo hayom: 'Why have you come home so early today?' Jethro's question to his daughters reveals the normal situation: the well is contested, the women are routinely driven away. Moses's intervention was exceptional — not merely polite but decisive. The same word madua (why) opens the commission narrative in Exodus 3:3 (madua lo-yivar hasneh, why is the bush not burning up) — the question that opens perception to the unexpected.
19: ish Mitsri hitstsileinu miyad haro'im: 'An Egyptian man delivered us from the hand of the shepherds.' The daughters identify Moses as Egyptian — he looks Egyptian, speaks Egyptian, acts with Egyptian authority. Moses is a man without a clear identity: Hebrew by birth, Egyptian by formation, stranger in Midian. The deliverer is always the outsider within — Joseph in Egypt, Daniel in Babylon, Christ in Judea (John 1:11: he came to his own, and his own people did not receive him).
20: ve'ayeh lamdua zeh azavtem et-ha'ish: 'Where is he? Why did you leave the man?' Jethro's characteristic hospitality — the expectation that a stranger who acts with such character should be invited to share the table. Kireu lo ve'yochal lachem (call him to eat bread): table fellowship as covenant-initiation. The shared meal is always a covenant act in the OT (cf. Genesis 31:54; Exodus 18:12). Moses enters Jethro's household through bread.
21: vayoel Mosheh lashevet et-ha'ish vayiten et-Tziporah bito leMosheh: 'Moses agreed to stay with the man, and he gave Moses his daughter Zipporah.' Vayoel (H2974, agreed/consented): the verb suggests a deliberate choice — Moses makes Midian his home. The marriage to Zipporah grounds Moses in a new family before he returns to his first family. The name Tziporah (H6855) means bird — perhaps signaling freedom or swift movement. Moses's Midianite period is not wasted exile but essential formation.
22: vateled ben vayiqra et-shemo Gershom ki amar ger hayiti be'eretz nochriya: 'She bore a son, and he called his name Gershom, saying: I have been a stranger in a foreign land.' Gershom (H1648): word-play on ger (H1616, stranger/sojourner) + sham (there) — stranger-there. Moses names his son after his own identity crisis: the deliverer of Israel is himself a displaced person, a stranger. The ger identity will shape Israel's ethics: you shall not oppress a stranger, for you know the soul of the stranger, having been strangers in Egypt (Exodus 23:9).
23: vayehi vayamim harabim hahem vayamot melek Mitsrayim vayane'anchu bnei Yisrael min-ha'avoda vayizaqu: 'During those many days the king of Egypt died, and the Israelites groaned because of their slavery and cried out.' Na'aqah (H5009, groan/cry out): the deep involuntary cry of those crushed beyond endurance. Tzaaq (H6818, cry out): the urgent legal cry to a superior for relief. Both words together emphasize the totality of Israel's anguish — involuntary suffering-cry and deliberate appeal. The Exodus begins with a groan that ascends to heaven.
24: vayishma Elohim et-na'aqatam vayizkor Elohim et-berito et-Avraham et-Yitzchak ve'et-Yaakov (wayyišmaʿ ʾĕlōhîm ʾet-naʾăqātām wayyizĕkor ʾĕlōhîm ʾet-bĕrîtô ʾet-ʾabrāhām ʾet-yiṣḥāq wĕʾet-yaʿăqōb): 'God heard their groaning and God remembered his covenant with Abraham, with Isaac, and with Jacob.' The four verbs of divine response in Exodus 2:24-25: heard (shama), remembered (zakar), saw (ra'ah), knew/took note (yada). Zakar (H2142, remember) is not mere mental recall but performative memory — God remembering the covenant is God activating it. The covenant made with three patriarchs is invoked with all three names: no generation of covenant is excluded.
25: vayar Elohim et-bnei Yisrael vayeda Elohim (wayyarʾ ʾĕlōhîm ʾet-bĕnê yiśrāʾēl wayyēdaʿ ʾĕlōhîm): 'God saw the children of Israel and God knew.' The verse ends simply: vayeda Elohim — and God knew. Yada (H3045, know/take note of) as covenantal recognition: YHWH does not merely observe Israel's condition but enters into it — knowing as intimate concern and committed response. The brevity of the verse amplifies its weight: the entire Exodus is set in motion by these four words. God's knowing is God's acting.
